title: Self-Correction, Distance Estimation and Local Testing of Codes
speaker: Dana Moshkovitz
abstract: We construct linear codes of almost-linear length and linear distance that can be locally self-corrected on average from a constant number of queries:1. Given oracle access to a word $w\in\Sigma^n$ that is at least $\varepsilon$-close to a codeword $c$, and an index $i\in [n]$ to correct, with high probability over $i$ and over the internal randomness, the local algorithm returns a list of possible corrections that contains $c_i$.

proof_stages.label: Coding-theoretic setup and target parameters
proof_stages.summary: The talk fixes notation for error-correcting codes, relative distance, rate, agreement, and the desired nearly linear length, near-one distance, and alphabet polynomial in 1/epsilon.

proof_stages.label: Local-code tasks and self-correction hierarchy
proof_stages.summary: The speaker defines local testing, distance estimation, local decoding, and self-correction, then strengthens self-correction to a two-sided/list-decoding and l-tuple formulation that implies distance estimation and local testing.

proof_stages.label: Reed-Muller baseline and its parameter obstruction
proof_stages.summary: Reed-Muller codes are reviewed as the classical source of local correction via random low-dimensional restrictions and low-degree testing, but their length/query/alphabet tradeoffs fail to achieve the desired simultaneous parameters, and worst-case local decoding faces known lower bounds.

proof_stages.label: Average-case self-correction as the achievable relaxation
proof_stages.summary: The problem is relaxed from correcting every coordinate with high probability to correcting a random coordinate with high probability, which avoids the worst-case local-decoding barriers while still sufficing for distance estimation and local testing.

proof_stages.label: PCP viewpoint and constrained-code hierarchy
proof_stages.summary: The construction is motivated by PCP query reduction: by adding a constraint phi and correcting only codewords whose messages satisfy phi, PCPs sit at the bottom of a hierarchy beneath local testing, distance estimation, and self-correction.

proof_stages.label: Composed outer-inner code with sampler neighborhoods
proof_stages.summary: The new code is built by taking a Reed-Muller-type outer code, reducing the degree of its query graph to polynomial in 1/epsilon, and replacing each outer-symbol neighborhood by inner encodings of the corresponding query views, with a sampler/expander property preserving distance.

proof_stages.label: Self-correction analysis of the composed code
proof_stages.summary: The proof of average-case self-correction has an easy completeness direction for close codewords and a harder soundness direction, implemented by correcting layers from multiple occurrences, retaining repeated list corrections, comparing shared block symbols, and using PCP-style inner constraints to force global consistency.

examples.name: Word split between two nearby codewords and garbage
examples.note: The speaker sketches a received word that agrees on different regions with codewords c1 and c2 and is garbage elsewhere to make concrete the list-decoding regime with multiple close codewords.
examples.engagement_reason: The speaker only sketches a possible received word lying partly near c1, partly near c2, and partly garbage to illustrate list decoding, without analyzing it further.

examples.name: Binary alphabet trivial output list
examples.note: The observation that over a binary alphabet one could trivially output both 0 and 1 motivates moving from single-symbol self-correction to self-correction of l-tuples.
examples.engagement_reason: The speaker applies the self-correction/list-output definition to the binary alphabet case, observing that returning both 0 and 1 makes one-symbol correction vacuous and motivating l-tuple correction.

passes.reason: the exhaustive list {0,1} over the minimal nontrivial alphabet makes one-symbol list self-correction vacuous
The full binary list {0,1} is a vacuous output used to show that single-symbol list correction over a tiny alphabet would be uninformative.
the list returning both binary symbols is a vacuous maximal output that makes one-symbol list correction trivial
the full list {0,1} over the minimal nontrivial alphabet is a maximal but vacuous output, showing naive one-symbol list correction can trivialize
Returning the exhaustive binary list {0,1} is a vacuous maximal output list, used to show that one-symbol list self-correction over a tiny alphabet would be trivial.

the exhaustive output list {0,1} over a binary alphabet makes one-symbol list correction vacuous

returning the full binary list {0,1} is a vacuous trivial output used to show one-symbol list correction over a binary alphabet is uninformative.

examples.name: Slightly corrupted word in the unique-decoding regime
examples.note: A word corrupted on only a small fraction of coordinates is used to illustrate why a naive average-case self-correction notion is not meaningful in the unique-decoding setting.
examples.engagement_reason: The speaker reasons through the unique-decoding/slight-corruption case, noting that if only about 1% is corrupted then reading the received word itself is correct with about 99% probability.

passes.reason: a standard small-error received word is used to show the proposed average-case notion is trivial in the unique-decoding regime
A slightly corrupted received word is used to show that the proposed average-case notion is trivial in the unique-decoding regime by simply reading the received symbol.
a lightly corrupted near-codeword is used to show the average-case notion becomes vacuous in the unique-decoding regime
a standard near-codeword received word, used to show the average-case notion becomes uninformative in the unique-decoding regime
A word with only a small fraction of corruptions is a standard unique-decoding input, used to show average-case correction can become trivial there.
a lightly corrupted word is used to show the average-case notion becomes too weak in the unique-decoding regime
a lightly corrupted unique-decoding word is invoked to show average-case correction would be trivially satisfied by the received symbol itself
A received word with only a few corruptions is used to show that average-case correction in the unique-decoding regime becomes nearly trivial by just reading the word itself.
a near-codeword with few corruptions shows the average-case unique-decoding notion can be satisfied trivially by reading the word itself
a lightly corrupted word is used to show the average-case notion becomes vacuous in the unique-decoding regime, while the word itself is ordinary

examples.name: Standard code concatenation analogy
examples.note: Classical outer/inner concatenation for alphabet reduction is presented as an analogy for the new composition construction.
examples.engagement_reason: The speaker traces the standard concatenation construction by encoding each outer alphabet symbol with an inner code and discusses how this reduces alphabet size and can be iterated.
